coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe based on CdSe quantum dots for the detection of Ag+ in environmental samples and living cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Zhiqiang
Cen, Jianfang
Jiang, Naijia
Sun, Yu
Li, Zhouyang
Yang, Liyun
coredata.dc:description: With the widespread application of Ag+ in modern life and industry, the potential hazardous effects of Ag+ to environment and humans have attracted great concerns. Thus, effective and rapid strategies for Ag+ detection are highly desirable. In this paper, a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor using CdSe quantum dots (QDs) has been constructed for sensitive and selective detection of Ag+, which is based on the formation of Ag2Se QDs. CdSe QDs were initially prepared and showed single wavelength emission at 510 nm. When Ag+ exists, a rising peak appeared at 650 nm and the emission at 510 nm declined, exhibiting distinct ratiometric fluorescence emission (I 650/I 510) characteristic with a linear response over the Ag+ concentration range of 0.01–4 μM. Significantly, the fluorescence changed from green to red. The detection limit of the constructed sensor is 1.4 nM. Furthermore, the sensing assay can be successfully applied to detect Ag+ in real water samples and living cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CdSe quantum dots
Fluorescence
Ratiometric detection
Silver ions

Mechanism for the detection of Ag+
Ag+ detection in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CdSe QDs. (a) TEM; (b) size distribution; (c) UV–vis and fluorescence spectra, the inset corresponds to the pictures taken under the sunlight and the UV lamp; (d) FTIR.
Influence of the concentration of CdSe QDs on Ag+ detection.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of CdSe QDs upon the addition of various concentrations of Ag+. (b) Plot of the fluorescence ratio I 650/I 510 versus the concentration of Ag+.
Fluorescence response of the CdSe QDs-based probe towards different metal ions.
XPS spectra of CdSe QDs in the presence of various concentrations of Ag+.
XRD spectra of CdSe QDs in the presence of various concentrations of Ag+.

Confocal laser scanning microscopy images of nanoprobe in living A549 cells. (a) A549 cells were incubated with CdSe QDs for 1 h. (b) A549 cells were incubated with 20 μL Ag+ (0.01 M) for 15 min and then treated with CdSe QDs for 1 h. Channel 1 (green channel), Channel 2 (red channel).
Schematic illustration for the ratiometric detection of Ag+ by CdSe QDs.
Comparison of methods for the determination of Ag+.
Determination of Ag+ in real water samples using CdSe QDs and ICP-MS.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe based on CdSe quantum dots for the detection of Ag+ in environmental samples and living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The detection of Ag+ is realized by transformation from CdSe quantum dots to Ag2Se quantum dots.

With the widespread application of Ag+ in modern life and industry, the potential hazardous effects of Ag+ to environment and humans have attracted great concerns. Thus, effective and rapid strategies for Ag+ detection are highly desirable. In this paper, a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor using CdSe quantum dots (QDs) has been constructed for sensitive and selective detection of Ag+, which is based on the formation of Ag2Se QDs. CdSe QDs were initially prepared and showed single wavelength emission at 510 nm. When Ag+ exists, a rising peak appeared at 650 nm and the emission at 510 nm declined, exhibiting distinct ratiometric fluorescence emission (I 650/I 510) characteristic with a linear response over the Ag+ concentration range of 0.01–4 μM. Significantly, the fluorescence changed from green to red. The detection limit of the constructed sensor is 1.4 nM. Furthermore, the sensing assay can be successfully applied to detect Ag+ in real water samples and living cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an important metal ion, silver ion (Ag+) has a broad utilization in the fields of electronics, photography and pharmaceuticals [1]. Notably, Ag+ shows excellent antibacterial property and numerous Ag-based agents have been developed as antibiotics [2,3]. However, excessive exposure to Ag+ may bring potential threats to environment and human health. Increasing evidences have shown that superfluous Ag+ may give rise to health problems, such as argyria, urine silver excretion, growth retardation, cardiac enlargement and liver damage [4-6]. According to the reports by the World Health Organization (WHO), the concentration of Ag+ in drinking water should be no more than 0.9 µM [7]. Thus, rapid, selective and sensitive detection of Ag+ is of vital significance.

In the past decades, methods like electrochemical assay [8], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [9] and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [10] have been successfully contributed to detect Ag+. However, the above techniques exhibit disadvantages of high time consumption, complicated sample preparation and costs of operation [11,12]. Due to the superiority in non-sample preparation, low cost and fast analysis, optical sensing technique is currently considered ideal candidate for sensitive detection of heavy metal ions [13-15].

Recently, fluorescent nanoprobes have attracted lot of interests in Ag+ detection. Xiao et al developed an “on–off” fluorescent assay for Ag+ detection using nitrogen, boron, sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NBS-CDs) [16]. whereas, the existence of Fe3+ could also lead to the effective fluorescence quenching of NBS-CDs. Based on the fluorescence quenching ability of Ag+ on copper nanoclusters (CuNCs), Zhang et al applied CuNCs for the detection of Ag+ in real water samples [17]. Although the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.08 nM, the detecting linear range (0.164–11.6 nM) is relatively narrow. Wang et al proposed a simple and sensitive method to determine Ag+ based on BSA coated CdTe QDs [18]. The LOD is as low as 10 nM. However, the detection was prone to be interfered by Cu2+ and Fe3+. Su et al synthesized carbon-doped silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs) and the prepared Si NPs could realize the selective detection of Ag+ and Hg2+ [19]. The LOD is 0.457 μM and 2.676 μM, respectively. However, high selectivity, sensitivity and wide linear range is hardly co-existent in most reported QDs-based Ag+ sensor.

Herein, we developed a novel fluorescence probe using CdSe QDs, which displayed a ratiometric fluorescence response to Ag+. Scheme 1 illustrates the principle of CdSe-based nanoprobe for Ag+ sensing. CdSe QDs exhibits strong fluorescence intensity at 510 nm and the addition of Ag+ gives rise to the formation of Ag2Se (a near-infrared QDs). Upon the addition of Ag+, the fluorescence intensity of CdSe QDs at 510 nm weakened, while that of Ag2Se around 650 nm enhanced. Thus, the constructed nanoprobe shows excellent selectivity and sensitivity for Ag+.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3, AR), selenium (AR) and N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NAC, 99%) were purchased from Aladdin. Sodium borohydride (greater than98%) and cadmium dichloride dihydrate (CdCl2·2H2O) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. The water used in the experiment is secondary deionized water.

The fluorescence spectra were collected by the RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). The UV–vis spectra were recorded on the UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy of CdSe QDs were recorded by the FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA). The TEM images of CdSe QDs were obtained from a JEM-2100(HR) electron microscope (Hitachi) under the voltage of 200 kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CdSe QDs before and after the addition of Ag+ was acquired using the X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XSAM800, Kratos). The ICP-MS measurement was carried out using NexlON300D (PerkinElmer, USA).

Se precursor solution was prepared by dissolving NaBH4 (80 mg) and Se powder (79 mg) in 4 mL ultrapure water. After stirring for 1 h, the mixture solution turned transparent and NaHSe solution was obtained. Cd precursor solution was prepared through dissolving NAC (55 mg) and CdCl2·2H2O (76.8 mg) in 160 mL ultrapure water with the adjusted pH of 9.0. Then, 340 µL NaHSe solution was injected to the Cd precursor solution and mix under stirring for 2 h at room temperature. Then, the mixture solution was transferred into a three-neck flask and the fabrication process was carried out at 100 °C with the protection of N2 flow. After 6 h, CdSe QDs with green emission were prepared. The purified CdSe QDs were obtained through precipitating the mixture solution with ethanol and centrifugation for 3 times. The final product was kept in 4 °C before experimental operation. The molar concentration of CdSe QDs is calculated according to the literature report [20].

The fluorescent detection assay was conducted in water solution at pH = 9.0. In a typical process, purified CdSe QDs was dispersed in the water solution with a concentration of 0.34 μM. Then, varied amounts of Ag+ were titrated into the CdSe QDs solution gradually and the corresponding fluorescence spectra were recorded from 450 nm to 900 nm. The fluorescence intensity of CdSe QDs at 510 nm and 650 nm were marked as I 510 and I 650, respectively.

To verify the selectivity of CdSe QDs-based ratiometric nanoprobe for the specific recognition of Ag+, the fluorescence spectra of the nanoprobe exposed to Ag+ (10 μM) and 100 μM of other interfering metal ions (Pb2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Na+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Fe3+, K+ and Mg2+) were recorded similarly as mentioned above and the interference of other metal ions was investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ag+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The real water samples were obtained from Yongjiang and Nanhu and filtered with 0.22 μm syringe filters before the detection experiment. Subsequently, standardized Ag+ solution was added into the above real water samples to prepare stock solutions with various concentrations of Ag+. Afterwards, the content of Ag+ was measured using CdSe QDs-based ratiometric nanoprobe.

Human lung adenocarcinoma A549 cells were seeded in glass bottom culture dishes and cultured overnight in DMEM medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) in 5% CO2 at 37 °C. Then, the medium was discarded and the cells were incubated with CdSe QDs for 1 h. Subsequently, the cells were washed by PBS for 3 times and further treated with Ag+ (20 μL, 0.01 M) for 15 min. After the washing process, the cells were immediately visualized and photographed under a Leica SPE laser scanning confocal microscope. Under the excitation of 405 nm, green and red light channels were collected. Under the excitation of 532 nm, the bright field was collected.

TEM, FTIR, UV–vis and fluorescence measurements were conducted to characterize the as-prepared CdSe QDs. As shown in Fig. 1 a, CdSe QDs are spherical in shape and show good dispersity. Additionally, the average size of CdSe QDs is 2.3 ± 0.8 nm (Fig. 1b). Fig. 1c depicts the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of CdSe QDs. The absorption band and fluorescence peak of the prepared CdSe QDs locate at 478 nm and 510 nm, respectively. The fabricated CdSe QDs exhibit strong green fluorescence under the excitation of UV lamp, indicating the successful preparation of fluorescent CdSe QDs. The FTIR spectra were applied to confirm the structure of superficial ligand of CdSe QDs. As shown in Fig. 1d, the peaks at 1569 cm−1 and 1396 cm−1 are asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxyl groups, respectively. In addition, NAC presents the stretching bond of the sulfhydryl group at 2536 cm−1, while the absence of the absorption band of sulfhydryl group is observed in CdSe QDs, revealing the attachment between the superficial Cd atoms and thiols of the CdSe QDs by covalent bond [21]. All the above results demonstrate the successful preparation of NAC-CdSe QDs.

Previous studies have shown that the concentrations of nanoprobe, pH of the solution and detection time could effectively influence the sensing results of nanoprobe for analyte [22,23]. Thus, the optimization of the above experimental conditions is of great necessity for improving the sensing behavior of nanoprobe. Fig. 2 a shows the effect of the concentration of CdSe QDs on the ratiometric detection of Ag+. The value of I 650/I 510 gradually increases in the concentration range of 0–0.34 μM and then decreases in the range of 0.34–0.81 μM, indicating the optimal probe concentration for detecting Ag+ is 0.34 μM. Besides, the effect of pH was investigated. At acidic condition, the protonation of the carboxyl group weakens the electrostatic attraction between CdSe QDs and Ag+. Alkaline environment could promote the deprotonation of carboxyl group and strengthens the interaction between Ag+ and CdSe QDs, while highly alkaline environment leads to the formation of Ag2O. As depicted in Fig. S1, the value of I 650/I 510 varies at different pH and reaches its maximum at pH = 9. Under the optimal concentration of probe and pH, the reaction time has been explored. As shown in Fig. S2, the value of I 650/I 510 increases dramatically as soon as Ag+ was added and shows a nearly constant when the reaction time is 8 min.

Under the optimal conditions, the sensitivity of CdSe nanoprobe for Ag+ detection was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the green fluorescence of the nanoprobe at 510 nm gradually decreases after the addition of increasing concentrations of Ag+. Meanwhile, a new fluorescence peak appears at the longer wavelength. The fluorescence intensity and location varied dramatically with the elevated concentrations of Ag+. At lower concentrations of Ag+, the fluorescence intensity at 650 nm increases and possess a red shift after the addition of Ag+, while that decreases at higher concentrations of Ag+, which is ascribed to the formation of Ag2Se [24]. Fig. 3b depicts the change of the fluorescence ratio (I 650/I 510) with different concentrations of Ag+. The result shows that the I 650/I 510 is linearly proportional to the concentration of Ag+ between 0.01 and 4 μM. The LOD for Ag+ is 1.4 nM (3σ), which is much lower than most of reported LOD based on fluorescent QDs for rapid detection of Ag+ (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of CdSe for Ag+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe for Ag+, the fluorescence response of the probe toward Ag+ and various interfering ions were explored under the optimized experimental conditions. The value of I 650/I 510 of CdSe QDs upon the addition of various metal ions is shown in Fig. 4 . Obviously, only the addition of Ag+ could generate the dramatically increase in I 650/I 510, while other metal ions barely influence the value of I 650/I 510, indicating that the ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe (CdSe QDs) exhibits excellent selectivity towards Ag+ detection, which may be due to the fact that the activation carrier of the reaction CdSe → Ag2Se is much lower than that of the reaction CdSe → MSe (M = like Pb, Cu and Zn) [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the detection of Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Ag+ by CdSe QDs is realized through the transformation of green emissive CdSe QDs to near-infrared Ag2Se QDs. According to the report, the cation exchange reaction of CdX (X = S and Se) QDs involved in the transformation process could occur at room temperature, indicating the applicability of CdSe QDs for Ag+ detection [31]. With the addition of Ag+, the fluorescence intensity of CdSe at 510 nm (I 510) decreases dramatically and a new fluorescence peak appears at longer wavelength (Fig. 3a). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of CdSe QDs at 650 nm (I 650) increases and the fluorescence peak exhibits a red shift after the addition of Ag+. The appearance and red shift of the new fluorescence peak at 650 nm should be ascribed to the transformation of CdSe QDs to Ag2Se QDs. According to the decrease in I 510 and increase in I 650, the ratiometric fluorescent probe for Ag+ detection is established.

To further demonstrate the mechanism for Ag+ detection based on CdSe QDs, XPS and XRD measurements were carried out. The elemental composition of CdSe QDs before and after the addition of Ag+ is investigated by XPS. As shown in Fig. 5 , Cd 3d and Se 3d peaks are observed in the full spectrum of CdSe QDs and the addition of Ag+ leads to the appearance of Ag 3d peak. Furthermore, the intensity of Ag 3d peak increases and the intensity of Cd 3d peak decreases with the growing amount of Ag+ (Fig. S3), suggesting the transformation from CdSe QDs to Ag2Se QDs.

The XRD results of CdSe QDs with the existence of varied Ag+ are depicted in Fig. 6 . For CdSe, the X-ray diffraction peaks of 2Θ at 25.2°, 41.9° and 49.3° degrees can be indexed with the characteristic peaks of (111), (220) and (311), reflecting the cubic crystal structure CdSe QDs [32]. As soon as Ag+ is added, the intensity of the characteristic peaks of (111), (220) and (311) decreases. When the molar ratio of CdSe:Ag+ is 1:40, the characteristic peaks of CdSe QDs disappears, accompanied by the appearance of (200) and (211) of Ag2Se, suggesting the generation of Ag2Se QDs.

From the XPS and XRD results of CdSe QDs with the existence of various concentrations of Ag+, it is definite that the addition of Ag+ into CdSe QDs leads to the transformation from CdSe QDs to Ag2Se QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ag+ detection in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the feasibility of CdSe QDs nanoprobe for Ag+ monitor in real water samples, various amounts of Ag+ were added into real water samples and CdSe QDs were subsequently added. The fluorescence spectra of CdSe QDs after incubating with Ag+ were collected and the concentration of Ag+ in water samples was calculated according to I 650/I 510. As shown is Table 2 , the results analyzed by ICP-MS and CdSe-based nanoprobe are very close and the recovery is in the satisfying range. The recovery for Ag+ detection is in the range of 96.3%-103.9%, indicating the applicability of CdSe-based nanoprobe for Ag+ detection in real water samples.

To further investigate the applicability of the established probe for monitoring Ag+ in living cells, Ag+ was introduced into the CdSe QDs pretreated A549 cells. The confocal microscopy images of CdSe QDs pretreated A549 cells in the absence and presence of Ag+ were acquired by collecting dual emission channels under the excitation of 405 nm. As shown in Fig. 7 , only green fluorescence in channel 1 is observed in CdSe QDs pretreated A549 cells. With the addition of Ag+, the intensity of green fluorescence decreases, while the red fluorescence intensity in channel 2 increases, suggesting that CdSe QDs can be used for intracellular determination of Ag+.

In summary, CdSe QDs with green fluorescence were successfully prepared and exploited to construct the ratiometric fluorescence sensor for detecting Ag+ (with high selectivity and sensitivity). The sensing system is based on the formation of near infrared Ag2Se QDs after the addition of Ag+ to CdSe QDs. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of CdSe QDs changes from green to red in the presence of Ag+, providing a direct observation under UV lamp. The ratiometric fluorescence emission characteristics (I 650/I 510) show a good linear relationship in the range of 0.01–4 μM with a low LOD of 1.4 nM. The constructed ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe displays excellent performance toward Ag+ detection with high sensitivity and selectivity. Furthermore, the strategy can be applied to determine the Ag+ in real water samples and living cells.

Zhiqiang Zhou: Investigation, Project administration, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Jianfang Cen: Investigation, Data curation. Naijia Jiang: Resources. Yu Sun: Formal analysis. Zhouyang Li: Data curation. Liyun Yang: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Nature Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22163004), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (2021GXNSFBA220060), Guangxi Scientific and Technological Development Projects (Guike AD20159068, Guike AD20297042), Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Chemical Materials and Devices of Ministry of Education (Jianghan University, JDGD-202013).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.122302.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
